review_text: I started Data Structures while juggling a full-time job and somehow got through, though I fell behind around deadlines and barely caught up; Prof. Haddad’s conceptual explanations helped me see the big picture, and the prerequisites fit better than I expected, even if the examples were thin.

Calculus II with Prof. Rios was intense and left me conflicted: I had a rough midterm crunch, and the project-heavy setup felt like it demanded more tool setup for payoff than I’d expected, even though the prerequisites hold up.

This Intro to Computer Networks course felt like it started easy but ramped up fast, and I’m mid-semester feeling the crunch from all the deadlines stacking up. I relied mostly on recordings and notes, since the tool setup and the frequent tool quirks ate into learning time more than they helped, and I remember that first week confusion about subnets that colored the rest of the term. Grading comments show detail, but they come later than I’d like, and the heavy assignment rhythm didn’t help my pace.

Data Structures in my first semester with Dr. Chen was solid for filling gaps I had, and the quick feedback helps adjust fast, but the email replies drag when deadlines hit; I started engaged, attendance faded, and office hours felt like a lifeline after a tough bug in week 6.

I picked Database Systems mainly to fit my evenings, studied around short sessions, and felt the workload ramp up fast. The software stack started rough but got manageable; exams felt fair, though feedback varied and the pace stayed uneven week to week. Overall, I’d recommend with preparation.

Honestly, I’m new to the discipline and the basics were already a lot to juggle, but this course felt like the required checkpoint I had to clear for the track. I kept up with nearly every lecture, and peer discussions sometimes helped more than the official materials, which was both reassuring and a little frustrating when the answers came too late or didn’t fit my questions. The toolset started rough for me—yes, the software stack was clunky at first—but I managed to get through it, and the later work felt more doable, if not elegant. There was a late-semester turning point that finally clicked, and I realized the prerequisites fit me better than I expected, even as I doubted the pacing and the sparse feedback until it was nearly over.

Taking Linear Algebra felt like following a path I was curious about, and the workload stayed doable the whole way, even when I fell behind a bit and had to sprint to catch up before deadlines. The slides were serviceable but not memorable; I’d glance at them and move on, which left me relying more on classmates for intuition than on lecture magic. I’m comfortable with proofs, but I’m not a systems person, and this course somehow filled some gaps I didn’t know were gaps, better than I expected for a mid-program core class. The way people helped each other in discussion posts and study groups mattered more to me than the staff replies, which felt a bit slow or off sometimes. There was a concrete project where we had to sanity-check a matrix factorization idea, and that tiny thing stuck with me as a real moment of seeing how the theory translates, even if the pacing and communication around policies were sloppy at times. Overall, I’m not sure I’d say yes to recommending it outright, but the curiosity carried me through more than the polish did.

I’m a returning student who feels rusty, juggling a full-time job and the OS course with Prof. Haddad, and I still remember that early confusing moment in week 2 when the controller lab kept giving me garbage on the first run—it shaped how I read the rest of the term. The lectures were clear overall and paced fine, but the platform quirks and a lot of instructions left too much room for interpretation, which made the admin side feel like a maze. I found the peer discussions more helpful than the official materials, which is nice because I could vent with folks who were also trying to squeeze study time into errands and meetings. The prerequisite fit felt solid, and I could tell the track expects the basics, even if I didn’t love the randomness of some deadlines and the weird grading quirks on the project rubric. I’m not sure whether I’d recommend it yet, because I’m still weighing whether the learning momentum outweighed the stress of platform quirks and the occasional vague instruction. But I did learn a lot, even if I didn’t enjoy the ride as a whole.

Machine Learning with Dr. Park was rough at first, but I stuck with the materials when I needed them and found the lectures clear enough to keep moving, even though I ended up failing the course while still feeling mixed about it. The software stack was a pain at the start, and some forum replies felt dismissive, but the office hours with one specific meet-up helped me finally sort out a model that produced decent results on a small project.

Overall, I’d say there’s a sense that this OS course is fine enough to finish, but the organization feels a bit messy for someone juggling a couple of other demanding classes in the final stretch before graduation. Prof. Haddad’s recorded lectures are handy when I’m rusty, but they’re pretty dry, and I end up rewatching sections just to catch a detail for a quiz that weighs heavily. The prerequisites hold up—what we build on makes sense—yet the collaboration expectations feel unclear, so I end up clarifying with teammates only after an awkward start. A single project about a small kernel module stands out, and it’s the most memorable bit, though the pace and cadence of deadlines in the term pattern weren’t helpful for planning. It’s usable, just not particularly smooth.

I took Linear Algebra with Dr. Miller while working full-time, and the practical steps—like applying vector spaces to real problems—felt really useful, even as the early confusion about eigenvectors stuck with me the rest of the term. The LMS and submission flow stayed smooth, but feedback came too late to adjust for the next deadline stacked up on me; plus the few assessments mattered a lot, which made the whole pace feel intense. I relied on fellow students more than staff sometimes, and I remember one misstep on a project where I mixed up a row-reduction method and it colored how I approached everything else

The OS course in my last semester before graduating was exactly the mix of demanding and useful I expected, and I felt it in small, concrete ways more than in grand statements. The lectures were clear and kept a decent pace, which helped as the software stack started rough but became manageable after a few weeks, especially once I stopped fighting the toolchain and started using it with the TAs during office hours. I came in thinking it’d be a strong fit because I’d heard it’s useful for industry work, and that turned out to be true in a few practical moments—like dragging Git and a containerized testbed into the grading flow—though the grading itself was a thorn: the assignments piled up with tight deadlines and I never quite felt relaxed about a late submission. The prerequisite fit starts accessible but ramps up sharply, and I appreciated how the forums and quick TA replies helped when I hit a sudden roadblock during a midterm-like project. I compared it to the easier prerequisite and found this one more taxing, but the peer interaction stayed mostly neutral and the workload was manageable most weeks, all things considered. Overall, I liked the instructor, disliked the grading quirks, and kept a steady pace through a semester that balanced several hard courses.

I took Machine Learning with Dr. Park as a required course for my track, and I remember staring at an early confusion that set the tone for the term: the lectures felt fast and sometimes felt like I was supposed to “just get it,” which made me fall behind and then scramble to catch up around deadlines. The assessment mix seemed mismatched with what we actually covered in class, and I ended up with a grade that felt like a failure despite slogging through, with mixed feelings about the content itself. admin instructions left a lot of interpretation, so I spent extra time second-guessing whether I was doing things correctly, which didn’t help when I was juggling another hard class at the same time. The collaboration expectations were unclear, and I mostly worked solo, though I did a small project that felt out of sync with the lectures in tone and depth. On the bright side, it did fill some prerequisite gaps better than I expected, and there were moments where I learned a lot, even if the whole experience left me frustrated but fair about the realities of the course.

I’m in my last semester and juggling a full-time job, so I fell behind in the Computer Networks course and ended up sprinting to catch up around the deadlines, which left me with a nagging sense of churn rather than steady progress. Dr. Miller is approachable and the peer help in our cohort surprisingly carried more weight than any instructor response, which helped when I was stuck on the TCP congestion window timings. The assessment mix felt misaligned with the lectures: we had a heavy project component and a couple of quick quizzes that didn’t map neatly to what we’d covered in class, so I ended up guessing through some of the practical bits. Practical application was the hardest pill to swallow, especially since the tooling didn’t feel obviously user-friendly for someone who’s already steeped in adjacent material; I kept wishing there was a clearer roadmap showing how the lab tasks tied to real deployments. I did appreciate the group work when our team clicked, but relying on someone else to pull extra weight added stress given my time constraints. A late-semester turning point was the moment I realized I needed to prioritize the core concepts over trying to master every tool, and that realization came with mixed relief and frustration.

I took Computer Networks while juggling a full-time job and a couple of other demanding courses, so I was always chasing time and deadlines more than I’d like to admit. Prof. Rios’s lectures were clear and his pacing was okay, but the way assessments are designed in this course felt exhausting for someone who can only study in fits and starts—there’s a lot of everything piled into tight windows, and I often fell behind and then had to scramble to catch up right before deadlines. The main thing that saved me was his office hours; when I could actually drop in, the quick explanations helped a lot, even if the written feedback afterward was pretty thin and didn’t always unblock the next step. The work is mostly individual, with little peer dependency, so there isn’t much room to bounce ideas around, which doesn’t help when you’re juggling time pressure and a rough mid-semester crunch. The one memorable week for me was the heavy midterm block where the format changed and I felt like the scores came fast but the reasoning behind them didn’t always show up clearly. Overall I’d say the course is fair in what it asks, but it wears you down if you’re trying to keep up with a full plate. I’d recommend it only if you’re prepared to put in extra time and leverage the professor’s office hours.

I started Machine Learning out of genuine curiosity, but the ramp-up felt sharp and the tooling setup was a hassle, so deadlines dragged during weeks with two hard courses. I kept up with nearly every lecture, yet an early confusion about gradients still sticks.

I needed Linear Algebra for my specialization, and it dragged despite some TAs being helpful. The slides are serviceable but not memorable, deadlines stacked up oddly, and the one big assessment felt pivotal—so I wouldn’t take it again, even though it helped.

Midway through the heavy stretch, Calculus II with Prof. Alvarez kept me on my toes while I juggled work; the workload felt fair but exhausting, especially with the report-heavy grading that dragged on, even though the LMS flow stayed smooth and I kept up with nearly every lecture. I started Linear Algebra as a total beginner and found the ideas click best when the instructor explains concepts, though the examples feel skimpy. Peer chats saved me when deadlines hit and I was juggling full-time work, but platform quirks added stress.

I took Computer Networks mainly because it was a required piece of the degree, and Dr. Chen’s intro sounded doable. I appreciated that the prerequisite fit felt solid for my background, and the peer discussions helped more than the official materials, which saved me a few late nights. The grading rubrics still felt vague, and the mix of assessments didn’t quite line up with what we actually saw in lectures, which left me guessing a bit about where to focus my effort. A concrete snag I remember is tooling glitches during deadline weeks that drenched our submission window—made it feel like the clock was always ticking. I managed the workload alongside a full-time job, and I did end up with a solid B that matched the effort I could commit, plus I remember office hours helping me puzzle through a tricky router-configuration example.

Linear Algebra was tough to fit around my full-time job, and I still ended up failing, even though I appreciated the strong conceptual explanations. The course leaned heavy on assignments with tight deadlines, and the feedback varied a lot—some comments helped, others felt generic. I’d compare it to another course from the term where they used clearer concrete examples, which made the material feel more approachable despite the tougher grade.

Linear Algebra in the middle of a heavy semester felt tougher than I expected, and the grind didn’t end up helping me with the big picture the way I hoped. I started out actually engaged, but by the second month my attendance faded a bit as the lectures dragged and the deadlines piled up, and I’m pretty sure the curve saved my grade more than any epiphany from the material. The platform quirks created more stress than they should—quirky submission windows and a few missing links that made simple tasks feel like guesswork, especially when I was juggling a rough mid-semester crunch. The prerequisites help a lot if you already know the adjacent material, but I found myself staring at some questions that assumed I did and realized I didn’t, which slowed me down more than I’d like. TAs were responsive and practical, which kept me from completely spiraling; their quick clarifications helped a lot on the last problem set, even if the instructions left too much room for interpretation in how to approach it. I recall an early confusing moment with eigenvectors that stuck with me and colored how I approached later topics, and that memory kept shaping how I tackled homework even as my focus drifted.

Summer term felt rushed, with fairly fair but exhausting assessments and a few uneven weeks; I kept up with almost every lecture and appreciated the smooth LMS flow, though the material itself was frustratingly dense and the tooling setup in week 3 was a real drag, especially for a short evening window.

I took Linear Algebra this summer while juggling a full-time job, and I’m glad I went in curious but rusty. The lectures were dry but the recorded ones helped me catch the ideas at my own pace, though feedback came sparsely and often felt late. Still, the collaborative student chatter carried me through, and the course did fill gaps better than I expected, especially around vector spaces, even if I wouldn’t say I’d wholeheartedly recommend it.

I’m wrapping up the last semester with Database Systems, and I’m not sure the grade reflects my own understanding after juggling work and two other tough courses. The recorded lectures help, but I found the collaboration rules and forums a bit murky, and mid-semester attendance faded for me.

I’m finishing Database Systems with Dr. Park in the last semester before graduation, mainly because it fit my schedule more than I was excited by the theory. The projects were actually where I felt I learned something practical—building queries and a small data model that I could extend after class—but the tooling setup was a pain, and those hiccups dragged deadlines out and made weeks feel longer. I’ve got a strong coding background and weak theory, so the prerequisite material built on what I knew, even if the pacing felt rushed when I started juggling this with another hard class. Feedback came fast enough to adjust, which helped, but I kept thinking I wouldn’t want to repeat this exact mix, even though the hands-on bits were useful. The biggest takeaway is that the tooling friction turned some weeks into battles, not wins.

I’m new to this stuff, and Cyber Security Basics started out rough because I kept tripping over a confusing early concept, which made the rest feel exhausting even though the lectures were clear and paced fine. The grading rubrics were vague, but I’d still recommend it to others starting out.

This term I treated Data Structures as my main focus since I was juggling a full-time job and only had a few evenings to study, and the pacing felt tighter because it was in the summer, but I actually appreciated the way the workload pushed me to stay on top of things. The organization was solid enough for me to rely on the weekly modules and the clear deadline pattern, though I found the prerequisite expectations a bit optimistic given my limited prior exposure; I still managed to keep up with the project-heavy setup by leaning on office hours and the TAs, which saved the course more than once. Recorded lectures were handy for catching up, even if they were somewhat dry, and the real value came from the quick forum replies and the rescue moments during office hours, where I could ask about a data structure trick I nearly botched on a timer. I’m in the C-ish range and unsure if that score reflects my understanding or the high-stakes exams, especially since the big project felt like the real test, not just quizzes, and I remember one session where we debated hash table collisions for 20 minutes.

took Computer Networks as my main focus this term, and its difficulty was tougher than I expected given my full-time job; i mostly relied on recordings and notes, and the tooling setup was frustrating. office hours helped once, but the forums could feel dismissive.

Linear Algebra was tough to fit around my full-time job, and I still ended up failing, even though I appreciated the strong conceptual explanations. The course leaned heavy on assignments with tight deadlines, and the feedback varied a lot—some comments helped, others felt generic. I’d compare it to another course from the term where they used clearer concrete examples, which made the material feel more approachable despite the tougher grade.

Midway through this heavy semester, the lectures sometimes feel uneven, but the LMS flow and submission felt smooth; I had enough time but motivation wobbled, and my strong coding but weaker theory background made the heavy report focus feel draining. Office hours helped, though.

I’m in my first semester here, juggling a full-time job, and Data Structures felt like it was designed around stacked deadlines more than a steady pace, which made sticking to a study plan tricky. The weeks vary a lot—one day we’re rushing through big-O proofs and the next we’re staring at pointer diagrams—so I end up second-guessing whether I should focus on the lecture slides or the assigned practice sets. The collaboration expectations are vague to me; it isn’t clear when we should pair up, and I’ve not felt confident about what my teammates are actually supposed to hand in. The course has only a few big assessments, but each one carries a lot of weight, which is fine in theory, but the grading feels opaque enough that I’m never sure what exactly to tighten up before the midterm. A standout thing is the occasional in-class hiccup—like a slide not loading, and then we spend five minutes on a different example—that small friction sticks in my memory. It works best if you’ve already seen some adjacent material, and I think I would’ve preferred a bit more steady delivery. Overall, it’s been calm but uneven, with a few concrete quirks that keep me from feeling fully confident.

Machine Learning with Dr. Park was rough at first, but I stuck with the materials when I needed them and found the lectures clear enough to keep moving, even though I ended up failing the course while still feeling mixed about it. The software stack was a pain at the start, and some forum replies felt dismissive, but the office hours with one specific meet-up helped me finally sort out a model that produced decent results on a small project.

Taking Cyber Security Basics with Prof. Haddad, I came in genuinely curious but it felt like the pace alternated between steady and sprint, especially in weeks with heavier labs. The group work helped when everyone pulled their weight, but the team I landed with didn’t always sync, which made some sessions drag. I’m new to the discipline and I think the course assumes a bit more prior knowledge than advertised, so I kept chasing basics while trying to keep up with tougher problem sets. The grading comments are thorough, but the turnaround is slow, which left me doubting where I stood. For example, the midterm format felt fair on paper but the questions leaned on topics I hadn’t quite mastered yet. Overall I learned a lot, even if I didn’t enjoy the ride.

Calculus II in the first term of the track felt like a needed stepping stone, and I did end up with a solid B that matched the effort I put in. The few assessments mattered a lot, and I appreciated the clear focus on the core ideas, though the instructor’s comments came late enough to blur some fixes before the next deadline. I’ll remember that initial push when the ramp-up started feeling sharp and a bit unsure, and how the project with the small graphing tool stuck with me after the early confusion.

I took Data Structures as an advanced elective while holding a full-time job, and I’m still not sure the grade I got reflected my understanding. The lectures felt steady enough, but I often relied on recordings and notes because I was juggling deadlines, so I can’t shake the sense that the pace and depth didn’t always line up with what I could actually absorb after a long day at work. The assessments felt a bit mismatched with the lectures—fast, surface-level quizzes followed by a big project, and the fast feedback with only a couple of lines explaining where I’d gone wrong didn’t help me connect the dots. Prerequisites seemed to build on what was promised, but the stack of deadlines stacked up in weeks with two big deliverables and a midterm week I didn’t see coming, which left me scrambling. I think the class works a lot better if you have a study group, because alone I struggled to pin down where the abstraction ends and the practical code begins. One memorable week was the project kickoff, when I realized I’d underestimated how much planning you need before you can even start coding, and that stuck with me long after the grade. Overall, I’m torn: I still won’t be certain I fully grasped the material, but I did finish with something I can point to as real progress.

I took Computer Networks hoping to finally scratch a curiosity about how the internet actually works, but I spent most of the term staring at dashboards and wondering why I wasn’t getting the intuition behind things like TCP handshakes. My strong coding background helped with some lab scripts, yet the theory felt slippery and the heavy project load plus high-stakes exams made it stressful to stay motivated. I did appreciate the clear lectures with decent pacing, but the feedback was sparse until late, and I fell behind around deadlines before finally catching up. One concrete moment that sticks is asking in office hours about congestion control and getting a quick, practical tip that I could apply in the next lab—small but memorable, and it didn’t fully fix my confusion. Overall, I’m not sure I’d recommend it, though it’s not a total write-off.

I took Computer Networks while juggling a full-time job and a couple of other demanding courses, so I was always chasing time and deadlines more than I’d like to admit. Prof. Rios’s lectures were clear and his pacing was okay, but the way assessments are designed in this course felt exhausting for someone who can only study in fits and starts—there’s a lot of everything piled into tight windows, and I often fell behind and then had to scramble to catch up right before deadlines. The main thing that saved me was his office hours; when I could actually drop in, the quick explanations helped a lot, even if the written feedback afterward was pretty thin and didn’t always unblock the next step. The work is mostly individual, with little peer dependency, so there isn’t much room to bounce ideas around, which doesn’t help when you’re juggling time pressure and a rough mid-semester crunch. The one memorable week for me was the heavy midterm block where the format changed and I felt like the scores came fast but the reasoning behind them didn’t always show up clearly. Overall I’d say the course is fair in what it asks, but it wears you down if you’re trying to keep up with a full plate. I’d recommend it only if you’re prepared to put in extra time and leverage the professor’s office hours.

Took Database Systems with Dr. Singh and it fit my short evening study blocks, solid B kind of matched the effort I could put in. The workload felt doable and the peer chats helped more than the official readings, though the assessments were heavy on reports and the feedback came in late to actually help me time things. One memorable week was that messy data-model quiz, and I’ll admit the prerequisite groundwork paid off even if attendance drifted mid‑semester.

The OS course in my last semester before graduating was exactly the mix of demanding and useful I expected, and I felt it in small, concrete ways more than in grand statements. The lectures were clear and kept a decent pace, which helped as the software stack started rough but became manageable after a few weeks, especially once I stopped fighting the toolchain and started using it with the TAs during office hours. I came in thinking it’d be a strong fit because I’d heard it’s useful for industry work, and that turned out to be true in a few practical moments—like dragging Git and a containerized testbed into the grading flow—though the grading itself was a thorn: the assignments piled up with tight deadlines and I never quite felt relaxed about a late submission. The prerequisite fit starts accessible but ramps up sharply, and I appreciated how the forums and quick TA replies helped when I hit a sudden roadblock during a midterm-like project. I compared it to the easier prerequisite and found this one more taxing, but the peer interaction stayed mostly neutral and the workload was manageable most weeks, all things considered. Overall, I liked the instructor, disliked the grading quirks, and kept a steady pace through a semester that balanced several hard courses.

I’m a returning student who felt rusty, and I took Data Structures mainly because I heard it’s useful for industry work; Prof. Cohen’s lectures were clear and the pacing decent, but I used the materials only when needed, and the project-heavy setup with high-stakes exams kept me cautious.

Honestly, I barely passed the Machine Learning course while juggling a couple of tougher classes, but the difficulty level pushed me to actually learn something. The quick feedback helped me adjust mid-term, especially once I realized the prerequisites fit best if you already know adjacent material, though the delivery felt uneven week to week. The biggest turning point was at the end when a late-week deadline pattern finally clicked, and classmates leaned on each other more than the staff helped.

I’m in Linear Algebra and I’ll admit I started the semester pretty engaged, juggling it with a full-time job, but by midterm I was barely showing up—attendance slipped as the workload from the other heavy classes piled on. The grading felt transparent in the sense that you could see how just a few points on the problem set mattered, but the feedback never landed quickly enough to help the next week’s work, so I kept guessing how to adjust my approach. The tool setup for the course took way too much effort for the payoff; getting the matrix calculator and the data file imports to cooperate consumed a good chunk of study time that I didn’t have. It’s one of those courses that seems useful in theory, yet the actual experience wasn’t enjoyable—the content was there, but the vibe was more about grinding through the steps than any spark of insight. The support channel mostly devolved into peers helping each other, while the staff felt distant, and it often felt like you had to already know the answer to get a straight reply. I’d recommend it only if you’re pretty sure you’ll prepare hard and juggle the prerequisites on your own.

Taking Linear Algebra in my last semester before graduation turned out to be a mixed bag, honestly. Dr. Singh is clearly passionate, and I appreciated the way he explains intuition, but the clarity drifts whenever we hit the more abstract parts, especially when the lectures jump between vector spaces and the proofs without a steady through-line. The course feels very assignment-heavy with frequent deadlines, which suits my coding background, yet the feedback quality fluctuates a lot from one submission to the next, and some comments arrive so late that they don’t help with the next deadline. I’m not sure I’d recommend it to someone aiming for strong theoretical grounding, since the timing and specificity of feedback can be inconsistent, and the email support during peak weeks was painfully slow. There were moments where a single concrete example in class would have saved me hours, but instead I got generic notes that didn’t map to the particular mistakes I was making in the coding-style proofs I tried to draft. I liked the instructor, but the grading felt misaligned with how I learn, and by the end I wasn’t sure if I’d actually recommend the course to someone chasing a specialization.

I like that the course organized stuff well, but the peer discussion helped me more than the official materials, and timing on grading comments felt slow; still, Dr. Chen builds on the prerequisites, and the project on eigenfaces was memorable, even if motivation drifted.

Database Systems was my main focus this term, but wow, the tooling setup was a nightmare and left me scrambling right from week one. I liked the instructor and the high-stakes tilt of the exams, but the grading felt unfairly punishing, especially with the heavy report workload that sank me during midterm crunch. Office hours rescued the course for me more than once, and a late-night push to push code through the DB tooling finally clicked, even if I barely passed.

I’m halfway through Data Structures and I’m juggling it with a full-time job, so I only glance at the material when I need it, which makes the course feel scattered. The explanations are solid conceptually, but the examples are thin and the platform quirks stress me out more than they should. I did attend one office-hours session after a tough homework, and the quick clarifications helped a bit, but the timing of feedback still feels rushed.

picking up data structures in my first semester again felt it would click, and i stuck with it mostly because i heard it would help with industry work. the delivery is solid on the big ideas, but the examples feel a bit thin, so i spent more time chasing a concrete intuition on my own while juggling full-time work. deadlines are frequent and the work piles up, which is fine in theory but the collaboration expectations stayed murky and i ended up emailing for clarification more than once when a project snapped back with last-minute changes. a late-semester turning point came when i finally mapped a practical lookup table in a small project and realized i could actually see the utility, even if i was tired and a little resigned by then.

Machine Learning felt approachable at first, especially the lectures—clear and paced in a way that kept me from zoning out, even with a full-time job looming over my evenings. I’m new to the discipline and the basics were doable, but the software stack started rough for me, cranking up to manageable once I got a feel for the workflow; a couple of late feedback rounds didn’t help when a project skid needed a nudge. Attendance dropped mid-semester as the weeknights piled up, and I found the course works best if you’re in a study group, trading quick tips and code snippets. I’m torn about recommending it; I’m unsure if my understanding truly clicked, yet the pacing and organization did land some solid takeaways.

Calculus II was a required hurdle my last semester before graduation, and with my strong coding background but weak theory, I found the jump from the prerequisites mostly on track but not consistent enough week to week. Dr. Miller presents ideas, then some weeks it clicks and others it feels like we’re chasing the tail of a proof we didn’t quite build in class, which made the pace feel uneven. The LMS and submission flow were smooth, which helped when the deadlines piled up—like three problem sets and a quiz all due in one Thursday came as a surprise and I scrambled to keep up. The mix of assessments didn’t line up with the lectures, so I spent more time reverse-engineering what the grader wanted than applying the calculus ideas, a mismatch that frustrated me given I was hoping the notes would guide me more directly. Prerequisites built on themselves in a usable way, and the course did feel like it leaned on solid foundations, but the delivery left gaps where I wished the intuition had been clearer. If I compare it to another course from the same term, this one felt more arithmetic and less conceptual, and I wouldn’t take it again, even though I did walk away with a few practical takeaways.

I started Data Structures hoping it would be a gentle entry, but the material ramps up fast and I was left juggling basics while chasing tougher concepts. Prof. Haddad was clear in class, yet the instructions for assignments left a lot open to interpretation, which didn’t help when I was balancing another hard class. The few assessments felt high-stakes and I left with a solid B that reflected the effort I could muster, even though I’m new to the discipline and a bit unsure about the prerequisites. The group work

Data Structures ended up as the main focus this term because the schedule fit, and I rode a mid-semester crunch hard. The LMS and submission flow were smooth, but I wish the graders were faster—grading comments were detailed yet surprisingly delayed, which made deadlines feel longer. I kept up by hitting office hours and the TAs’ forum threads, especially when a tricky linked-list assignment dragged on; I’d still take it again, even though I’d avoid the same timing squeeze.

Data Structures with Dr. Chen felt like it teetered between solid ideas and a bit of a fog, especially for someone like me who comes from a strong coding background but weaker on the theory. The conceptual explanations were clear enough, but the examples often didn’t land, and the toolchain hiccups (deadlines sliding because the grading platform hiccuped midweek) made a rough mid‑semester crunch feel worse. The grading rubric was vague enough to leave me unsure if a midterm mistake would tank my grade, even though conceptually I understood most of the material; office hours rescued the course more than once, and I walked away with a few practical takeaways, like a clean way to reason about trees, even if the big picture still felt fuzzy. I’m not sure I’d recommend it, especially compared to easier prerequisites, but it wasn’t a total waste.

I’m in mid-program core courses, and Operating Systems with Dr. Park felt like a mix of solid material and stress I wasn’t expecting. The recorded lectures helped when I isolated a late-night doubt, but they’re pretty dry, and the platform quirks made a few submission steps feel unnecessarily fiddly. The report-heavy workload stacked up fast around exams, and a early confusion about process scheduling shaped how I approached everything after, even though I learned a lot. The course works best if you’re in a study group, and I wish the deadlines weren’t so clustered.

The Operating Systems course fit my evenings but the pacing in the summer term was brutal, with deadlines stacked up and the exams looming after a short, compressed block. Slides were serviceable but not memorable, and the feedback on assignments was all over the place—some were quick and helpful, others vague. It helps to study with a group, since solo work felt like dragging a ton of friction; I’d only recommend it if you’ve got that back-pocket study squad and aren’t aiming for top marks without some serious

Took Database Systems with Dr. Singh and it fit my short evening study blocks, solid B kind of matched the effort I could put in. The workload felt doable and the peer chats helped more than the official readings, though the assessments were heavy on reports and the feedback came in late to actually help me time things. One memorable week was that messy data-model quiz, and I’ll admit the prerequisite groundwork paid off even if attendance drifted mid‑semester.

Midway through a heavy semester, I’m juggling Linear Algebra mostly around short evening sessions, and Dr. Chen’s lectures are clear enough to keep me from completely spinning, with a decent pace that helps me stay afloat between work blocks. The forums are sometimes helpful, though I’ve noticed a few folks can be dismissive when a question sounds basic, which makes you hesitate to post. The assessment mix feels off from what we cover in lectures, and the tool setup is a hassle that eats into study time for a payoff that isn’t obvious, especially with deadlines stacking up. Still, the grading feels fair enough, even if it exhausts you, and I’m not sure I’d outright recommend it, given the uneven spots and the late-semester crawl.

I’m a returning student who felt rusty, so I came into Database Systems with a lot of questions, and I actually left with something usable, even if I’m still catching up in spots. Prof. Cohen explains the big-picture ideas clearly enough, but the week-to-week tooling glitches made the deadlines feel harsher than they should’ve been—our project repo kept hiccuping just as we hit a hard due date, which fk’ed my pacing more than once. The peer discussions in section helped a lot more than the official slides, and I found myself leaning on a few classmates to sanity-check the normalization questions and the ER diagrams we talked through in chat between sessions. The quick feedback from Prof. Cohen’s comments was a relief; I could adjust the next draft without waiting ages, even if I still wore out a coffee cup triaging the latest feedback and the new rubric tweaks. There was one concrete moment that sticks: a short chat after office hours where I struggled to justify a junction table choice, and the guidance felt practical, not just theoretical. Overall it’s a heavy, time-consuming track course, and I’m tired and resigned about catching up, but the discussion-heavy approach keeps it worth it.

I signed up for Operating Systems hoping to slot it around my short evening study sessions, and I did manage to fit in the lectures, labs, and a late-night debugging sprint here and there, but the rhythm felt uneven week to week. Prof. Cohen is clearly knowledgeable, and the prerequisite material did fill some gaps for me better than I expected, though the instruction delivery sometimes wandered and left me chasing what the point of a assignment or a slide was. The course leaned heavy on a few big projects and high-stakes exams, which pushed me to stay engaged early on, yet my attendance faded mid-semester as evenings got busy, especially after a few stretches where the lectures didn’t connect with the weekly lab tasks. Scores came back quickly, but the explanations were a little thin, so I walked away with a number and not a lot of actionable feedback. I learned a lot, but the whole experience wasn’t exactly enjoyable, and I still remember that early confusion about the memory hierarchy that colored my approach the rest of the term. Overall I’d probably recommend it to others who need the schedule fit, even if it’s not the smoothest ride.

Data Structures fit my halfway-through-mega-semester schedule, and I mostly skimmed when I could. The practical parts were helpful, but the workload and late feedback varied a lot, though office hours kept me from completely sinking.

Last semester in Linear Algebra, I kept up with nearly every lecture, but the tooling setup was a headache and the admin friction didn’t help, especially when I was juggling the last-minute access to the online quiz system. The few assessments mattered a lot, and though the recorded lectures were useful, they felt dry, which left me wishing for a study group to break things down; I had a late-night chat in office hours that finally clicked one concept, and it suggested my overall view is mixed and a bit fragile

This was my last semester before graduation, juggling a full-time job and not a ton of study time, so I wanted a course that could run on a tighter schedule and still push me. The Machine Learning class overall was okay, with the LMS and submission flow staying smooth enough that I didn’t get stuck fighting the basics every week. My big frustration was that feedback felt sparse until it was basically too late to apply it, which left me scrambling to salvage a few assignments after the deadline pattern had already shifted in practice. Office hours saved the course for me here and there, and the professor, Prof. Rios, was approachable, but I still left with a nagging sense that the policy communication was sloppy—important pivots or expectations didn’t land clearly until well after they mattered. A notable detail: the mid-program core vibe and the time pressure being manageable most weeks helped, but the mid-semester project deadline converging with other priorities created more stress than the grading quirks deserved. In the end, I weighed the tradeoffs—solid tooling and a reasonable pace against patchy early feedback and patchy communication—without a single tidy conclusion.

I took Operating Systems mainly to fit my schedule with a couple of other heavy courses, and I’ll admit the workload felt heavier than I expected, especially with the steady stream of assignments due every week. Prof. Cohen delivers clear lectures with decent pacing, and I appreciated how the prerequisite gaps got filled better than I anticipated, which saved me a lot of scrambling on fundamentals. The exam timing and structure felt fair enough, but the constant deadlines kept me perpetually chasing, so even when the questions were reasonable, I was spent by the end. I mostly relied on recordings and notes, and when group work clicked, it added real value; we had one solid teammate who kept us organized, which helped a bit with the more design-heavy parts. The collaboration can stall if the team doesn’t pull its weight, though, and that was frustrating when you’re juggling multiple demanding courses. I didn’t hate the content, and the interest shown in the material when a lab scenario clicked was tangible, but the pace and the quiz-load wore me down, leaving me wondering if the balance could be smoother.

I picked Database Systems mainly to fit my evenings, studied around short sessions, and felt the workload ramp up fast. The software stack started rough but got manageable; exams felt fair, though feedback varied and the pace stayed uneven week to week. Overall, I’d recommend with preparation.

Calculus II was a required hurdle my last semester before graduation, and with my strong coding background but weak theory, I found the jump from the prerequisites mostly on track but not consistent enough week to week. Dr. Miller presents ideas, then some weeks it clicks and others it feels like we’re chasing the tail of a proof we didn’t quite build in class, which made the pace feel uneven. The LMS and submission flow were smooth, which helped when the deadlines piled up—like three problem sets and a quiz all due in one Thursday came as a surprise and I scrambled to keep up. The mix of assessments didn’t line up with the lectures, so I spent more time reverse-engineering what the grader wanted than applying the calculus ideas, a mismatch that frustrated me given I was hoping the notes would guide me more directly. Prerequisites built on themselves in a usable way, and the course did feel like it leaned on solid foundations, but the delivery left gaps where I wished the intuition had been clearer. If I compare it to another course from the same term, this one felt more arithmetic and less conceptual, and I wouldn’t take it again, even though I did walk away with a few practical takeaways.

Last semester felt like a slog, but Prof. Cohen saved the course for me with his office hours—those quick one-off explanations rescued some tangled concepts when my full‑time job left me with barely any study time. The intro material was fine, but the examples were thin, and grading comments came late even though each check mattered a lot; I left feeling useful but not really enjoying it, especially compared to the other course from the term.

Taking Operating Systems in the middle of a brutal semester, I went in with genuine curiosity, but I got tangled in the week-by-week grind. I fell behind and caught up around deadlines, mostly by skim‑reading the official slides and then sprinting to discuss things in peer groups—that helped more than anything the official materials offered. Office hours with Prof. Haddad felt like a lifeline, almost a rescue—one night I brought a mess of notes and he helped me map what mattered for the next couple of weeks. The workload is report-heavy and time-consuming, and the feedback mindset felt sparse until it was already due, which was a bummer.

The Operating Systems course fit my schedule more than my interests, and being new to the discipline, I relied mostly on recordings and notes. The slides are serviceable but not memorable, and the workload felt heavy with frequent deadlines, especially during a rough mid‑semester crunch; the platform quirks added extra stress I wish were avoided. One concrete annoyance: the grader seemed to expect more prior knowledge than advertised, which didn’t help my first-semester learning pace.

Cyber Security Basics fit my curiosity, and the accessibility here was real—I could follow lectures okay, even when I was juggling full-time work. However, I felt the prerequisites assumed more background than advertised, and the materials didn’t click for me; some readings felt thin or out of date, and I often wished for clearer, step-by-step notes. Feedback varied a lot by assignment, which didn’t always help when I was chasing deadlines and fallen behind but managed to catch up around big due dates. I relied on office hours and the TA for quick sanity checks, and I appreciated the occasional focused discussion, even if instruction delivery felt uneven week to week. I’ll remember the late-life-cycle risk discussion more than the week-by-week grind.

Taking Cyber Security Basics with Prof. Haddad, I came in genuinely curious but it felt like the pace alternated between steady and sprint, especially in weeks with heavier labs. The group work helped when everyone pulled their weight, but the team I landed with didn’t always sync, which made some sessions drag. I’m new to the discipline and I think the course assumes a bit more prior knowledge than advertised, so I kept chasing basics while trying to keep up with tougher problem sets. The grading comments are thorough, but the turnaround is slow, which left me doubting where I stood. For example, the midterm format felt fair on paper but the questions leaned on topics I hadn’t quite mastered yet. Overall I learned a lot, even if I didn’t enjoy the ride.

Cyber Security Basics with Prof. Alvarez felt like a lot of noise and a few sharp edges. I’m in the intro service course mode, trying to fit short evening sessions around my job, and the platform quirks this term didn’t help—random login prompts during a sprint of assignments, and the quiz timer sometimes eating questions right as I hit submit. The grading rubric seems vague, so I’m unsure if my C reflects understanding or just how the rubric scraped the main points, and feedback quality really varied by assignment. Peer discussion helped more than the official materials, and I remember one office-hours chat where a quirky example about a phishing flow clicked for me, but otherwise I wasn’t convinced the tools were friendly. I wouldn’t take it again, even though a few moments did stick. The tradeoffs nag at me: it built a few instincts, but the friction and the late-night platform headaches wore me down.

Database Systems was oddly useful but not enjoyable for me. I had enough time, but my motivation was inconsistent, and the tool setup felt like a sink of effort with little payoff. The project-heavy pace and high-stakes exams kept me on edge, and communication felt sloppy at times, though the prerequisites helped when I had adjacent material to lean on.

still not sure why calculus II felt designed around printed deadlines more than learning, but i was juggling a full-time job and rusty instincts yes, so the pace hit me hard the first few weeks. Prof. Rios is approachable in office hours, and i appreciate that he answers concrete questions about confusing steps, like how to set up the u-substitution when the chain rule keeps tripping me up. the assignments pile up with tight deadlines, and instructions sometimes leave too much room for interpretation, which adds stress when you’re winging it after a long shift. group work helps when the team actually communicates, and i found myself relying on a teammate to sanity-check thecalculator steps on a midterm review.

I’m new to the discipline, so Data Structures felt like a steep starting line, especially since I’m trying to fit short evening study sessions around a rough midsemester crunch. The pacing felt mostly workable on weeks when the classes kept moving, but some weeks it dragged and others sprinted, which made it hard to keep a steady rhythm. The workload was doable if I chunked it into small nightly blocks, though the software stack started rough and then slowly became manageable once I got the hang of the basic tooling for the project. The grading rubric, though, always felt a bit vague to me—comments would be detailed but often arrived late, and I couldn’t tell how much emphasis was on the design choices versus the correctness. I did appreciate that one big project required actual code I could show in a portfolio mood, but the instruction delivery was uneven week to week, so I’d leave a lecture unsure what the core takeaway was supposed to be. Despite the messy start, I still think this course is a required hurdle for the track, and I’ll admit I left with real questions about where the fundamentals end and the optimization begins.

I’m a returning student who feels rusty, juggling a full-time job and the OS course with Prof. Haddad, and I still remember that early confusing moment in week 2 when the controller lab kept giving me garbage on the first run—it shaped how I read the rest of the term. The lectures were clear overall and paced fine, but the platform quirks and a lot of instructions left too much room for interpretation, which made the admin side feel like a maze. I found the peer discussions more helpful than the official materials, which is nice because I could vent with folks who were also trying to squeeze study time into errands and meetings. The prerequisite fit felt solid, and I could tell the track expects the basics, even if I didn’t love the randomness of some deadlines and the weird grading quirks on the project rubric. I’m not sure whether I’d recommend it yet, because I’m still weighing whether the learning momentum outweighed the stress of platform quirks and the occasional vague instruction. But I did learn a lot, even if I didn’t enjoy the ride as a whole.

Calculus II was rough mid-semester: I fell behind as deadlines piled up and the tools to set up our environment felt like a slog, but quick feedback helped me claw back enough to pass with a curve. The course works better with a study group, and staff support felt limited.

I started Computer Networks hoping for a solid theory base, but the lectures felt dry despite Prof. Haddad’s clear effort; the LMS flow was smooth, yet the exams like the midterm seemed out of sync with lectures, and I remember a helpful office-hour moment about a routing assignment that helped a bit.

The Data Structures course was a mixed bag: the tooling was solid and the online compiler finally felt usable, but the prerequisites hit hard and I spent more time chasing explanations in forums that were occasionally dismissive. Attendance mostly meant watching recordings and skimming notes, which helped since I’m comfortable with proofs but not big on systems work; the workload is light until the last project, where one brutal, last-minute deadline made the assessment feel fair but exhausting.

Calculus II felt like a semester in the middle of a flood season—heavy and fast, with a few bright pockets. I’m comfortable with proofs, but the course seemed to assume more background in systems-level thinking than what was advertised, which made some integration tricks and multi-step proofs land harder than they should have. The lectures sometimes felt uneven week to week, and I would grab a few slides or a quick demo when a topic clicked, then have to slog through the rest on my own, which didn’t help the overall organization. Office hours saved me more than once, though: when the curve looked grim, a couple of clarifications on a chain rule extension finally clicked, and I could get back on track for the next assignment. The feedback tended to come late enough to miss a chance to learn from a mistake, and the deadlines sometimes felt like they were juggling with the same late-night sprint energy as last minute test prep. One standout memory is the midterm week where a tough, open-ended problem finally forced me to write out a clean, logical argument rather than rely on a trick, and that moment stuck with me even as I compared this course to a different Calc II from the same term.

Taking Machine Learning mid-program core while juggling a couple other demanding courses worked OK for me, and I appreciated that the workload felt manageable most weeks even though it was heavy. I signed up mainly because the schedule fit my term better than because I was dying for the topic, and the course did build well on the prerequisites, which helped me not feel completely lost when we dove into the stats basics and gradient ideas. The assessments were mostly long report-style tasks that ate into my time, and I didn’t love that the collaboration expectations were a bit fuzzy—my group didn’t have a clear plan for who did what, so I ended up drafting more than I expected on my own. Recorded lectures were useful to retake, but they felt dry, like listening to a textbook rather than a discussion, which made it harder to stay engaged at times. I’m a bit unsure whether my C-grade reflects my understanding, since the final project and the reports were heavy on presentation and not as transparent about the reasoning steps I wanted to show. Overall I’d still recommend broadly, just not expect a breezy ride.
